title: Modal, Overlay & Confirm
intro: Overlays interrupt the page — so the only real design question is how much they should interrupt it. `wf-overlay` is the shared scrim-plus-container scaffold; inside it you place a centered `wf-modal` for focused tasks, an edge-anchored `wf-drawer` for adjacent context, or a `wf-modal-confirm` for destructive yes/no decisions. `wf-tooltip` is the unobtrusive cousin: a hover-only hint that never blocks anything and never carries anything critical.

blocks.body:
Every overlay starts from the same wrapper: a `wf-overlay` (fixed, full-viewport, `z-index: 1000`) holding a `wf-overlay-backdrop` scrim and one content surface. Adding `wf-overlay-visible` is what fades the scrim in (`opacity 200ms`) and animates the surface — a modal scales up from `0.95`, a drawer slides in from its edge. In a real prototype you toggle that class; in a static wireframe you simply ship it on.

The content surface is one of two shapes. `wf-modal` is a centered column (default `max-width: 500px`) with an optional `wf-modal-header`, a scrolling `wf-modal-body`, and a right-aligned `wf-modal-footer`. `wf-drawer` is the same header/body/footer column anchored to an edge — `wf-drawer-right` is the default and the one you'll reach for 90% of the time.

`wf-modal-confirm` is not a separate component — it's a modifier on `wf-modal wf-modal-sm` that center-stacks an icon, title, and message for destructive or irreversible decisions. Reach for it instead of hand-building a Modal whenever the answer is a simple yes/no with consequences. `wf-tooltip` lives entirely outside this overlay machinery: it's an absolutely-positioned bubble inside a `wf-tooltip-container`, shown on `:hover` or with the `wf-tooltip-visible` class for wireframes.

blocks.title: Choosing the overlay — match cognitive load, not visual prominence
blocks.body:
§4.2 of DESIGN.md calls this the spot where the most drift happens. The rule is to match the overlay to how much the interaction should cost the user, not how important it looks:

Destructive or irreversible confirm? → `wf-modal-confirm`. Focused task that should block the page? → `wf-modal`. Adjacent context that shouldn't block? → `wf-drawer`. One-line hover hint? → `wf-tooltip`. (Transient feedback is a `wf-toast`; persistent inline state is a `wf-alert` — both covered in their own entries.)

The distinction that matters most: a Modal blocks and traps; a Drawer is the default for "let me look at this without losing the page."

blocks.title: Modal variants
blocks.caption: Centered modals over a dimmed scrim: header (title + close), scrolling body, and a right-aligned footer action row. This is the focus-trapped, center-stage branch of the overlay decision tree — use it sparingly, every modal is a context switch.

blocks.title: Modal sizes & structure
blocks.intro: One size modifier per modal; default is 500px. Header and footer are both optional — compose only the regions you need.

rows.name: wf-modal
rows.when: Default focused task, 500px wide. Edit a schedule, configure an alert.
rows.code:
Modal Title

Modal content goes here.

rows.name: wf-modal-sm
rows.when: Compact 400px — simple confirmations and short forms. Also the base for confirm dialogs.

rows.name: wf-modal-lg
rows.when: 700px — a task with more fields or a wider table.

rows.name: wf-modal-xl
rows.when: 900px — dense multi-column editing.

rows.name: wf-modal-full
rows.when: Near-fullscreen (viewport minus 48px) for immersive editors.

rows.name: header / body / footer optional
rows.when: Drop the footer for a header-only info modal, or drop the header for a body-only modal with inline actions.
rows.code: A minimal modal with inline actions.

blocks.title: Drawer — right
blocks.caption: A right-edge slide-in drawer over a scrim, with the same header / scrolling body / footer column as a modal. This is the side-panel branch of the overlay decision tree — the default for adjacent context that shouldn't block the page.

blocks.title: Drawer directions & widths
blocks.intro: A drawer combines a direction class (where it anchors) with an optional width class (left/right only). Default direction is right; default width is 600px. Each direction animates via a transform that `wf-overlay-visible` resets to zero.

rows.name: wf-drawer-right
rows.when: Default. Slides in from the right edge — row details, filter panels, activity logs.
rows.code:
Drawer Title

Drawer content goes here.

rows.name: wf-drawer-left
rows.when: Anchors to the left edge — secondary navigation or a tree.

rows.name: wf-drawer-bottom
rows.when: Slides up from the bottom (max-height 80vh) — action sheets, mobile-friendly pickers.

rows.name: wf-drawer-top
rows.when: Slides down from the top — global notices or a command palette.

rows.name: wf-drawer-full
rows.when: Full-screen takeover with no border or radius.

rows.name: wf-drawer-narrow
rows.when: 400px-wide left/right drawer — filters or settings.

rows.name: wf-drawer-wide
rows.when: 800px-wide left/right drawer — detail panels with more content.

blocks.title: Confirm dialog variants
blocks.caption: Compact ConfirmDialogs built from wf-modal-sm + wf-modal-confirm: a colored circular icon, a title, a one-line message, and a centered Cancel + commit footer. Danger commits get wf-button-danger; the icon color (danger / warning / info / success) signals consequence.

blocks.title: Confirm dialog icon intent
blocks.intro: `wf-modal-confirm` center-stacks the body and centers the footer. The `wf-confirm-icon` color carries the consequence — pair a danger icon with a `wf-button-danger` commit so color, shape, and label all agree.

rows.name: wf-confirm-icon-danger
rows.when: Destructive, irreversible commits — Delete, Remove. Red. Pair with wf-button-danger.
rows.code:
⚠

Delete Contract?

This action cannot be undone. All associated data will be permanently removed.

rows.name: wf-confirm-icon-error
rows.when: Alias of danger — identical red treatment. Use whichever name reads clearer.

rows.name: wf-confirm-icon-warning
rows.when: Reversible-but-lossy decisions — Discard changes, Archive. Amber.

rows.name: wf-confirm-icon-info
rows.when: Consequential but neutral — Send notification, Sign out. Blue.

rows.name: wf-confirm-icon-success
rows.when: Affirmative milestone — Ready to publish? Green.

blocks.title: Tooltip positions
blocks.caption: Always-visible tooltip previews in top / bottom / left / right placements — a dark neutral-900 bubble with a rotated-square arrow. A lightweight hover hint, never a place for critical information.

blocks.title: Tooltip placement & modifiers
blocks.intro: A tooltip is a `wf-tooltip` plus a placement class, nested in a `wf-tooltip-container` next to its trigger. It shows on `:hover` of the container; add `wf-tooltip-visible` to pin it open for static wireframes.

rows.name: wf-tooltip-top
rows.when: Above the trigger. The most common placement for buttons in a toolbar.
rows.code: This is a tooltip

rows.name: wf-tooltip-bottom
rows.when: Below the trigger — when there's content above it.

rows.name: wf-tooltip-left / wf-tooltip-right
rows.when: Beside the trigger — for elements at the top or bottom edge.

rows.name: wf-tooltip-visible
rows.when: Pins the tooltip open. For wireframe screenshots only — never simulates real hover behavior.

rows.name: wf-tooltip-multiline
rows.when: Longer hints that need to wrap (max-width 200px, centered). Default tooltips are single-line nowrap.
rows.code: A longer description that wraps to multiple lines.

blocks.title: Overlay tokens
blocks.intro: The values that define an overlay's scrim, surface, and stacking. All resolve through tokens/index.css.

rows.use: The scrim color behind every modal and drawer — a 50% neutral-900 dim.
Modal, drawer, and tooltip-free surface background.
Modal/drawer border and the header/footer divider lines.
Corner radius on modals and on the open corners of edge drawers.
The focus state on wf-modal-close and wf-drawer-close — never outline:none alone.
Tooltip text on the dark bubble.

blocks.title: Confirm icon tokens
blocks.intro: The 48px circular wf-confirm-icon uses a tinted background plus a saturated foreground per intent. The foregrounds are the standard status colors; the tints are baked literals in feedback.css.

rows.use: Danger/error icon foreground on a #fef2f2 tint.
Warning icon foreground on a #fffbeb tint.
Info icon foreground on a #eff6ff tint.
Success icon foreground on a #f0fdf4 tint.
Makes the wf-confirm-icon a perfect circle.

blocks.title: Loading state inside an overlay
blocks.code: Deleting...
blocks.note: wf-loading works inside any modal or drawer body — show it after the user commits, replacing the footer buttons so they can't double-fire the action.

blocks.title: Overlay choice (DESIGN.md §4.2)
blocks.intro: Match the overlay to the cognitive load of the interaction, not to how prominent it looks. These are the load-bearing distinctions.

items.rule: Destructive or irreversible confirm → wf-modal-confirm, always. Never a generic Modal.
items.why: The center-stacked icon-title-message shape is part of the safety guarantee — it reads as a decision, not a form.

items.rule: Focused task that must block the page → wf-modal.
items.why: A modal centers, scrims, and traps focus. Use it sparingly: every modal is a context switch.

items.rule: Adjacent context that doesn't need to block → wf-drawer.
items.why: It's the default for 'look at this without losing the page' — the underlying page often stays scannable behind it.
Tooltips are hover/focus-only hints; anything the user must act on belongs in an Alert or Modal.

items.rule: If the user must commit and cannot interact with the page behind, it's a Modal, not a Drawer.
items.why: A Drawer implies the page is still live; using one for a blocking commit misleads the user about what's still clickable.

items.do: <div class="wf-modal wf-modal-sm wf-modal-confirm"> for a delete
items.dont: <div class="wf-modal"> with a hand-built Cancel/Delete row
items.why: DESIGN.md §7.3: don't use a generic Modal for destructive confirmation. The confirm shape is the safety guarantee.

items.dont: Danger icon paired with a plain wf-button-primary commit
items.why: Color is the warning. A red icon over a blue commit button sends two different signals about how risky the action is.

items.do: Put the decision in a ConfirmDialog or Modal
items.dont: Put 'You have unsaved changes' only in a wf-tooltip
items.why: §4.2 / §7.3: tooltips are invisible to many users and inaccessible on touch — never the home for critical info.

items.do: wf-drawer-right for an interactive side panel
items.dont: wf-drawer for a commit the user must make before doing anything else
items.why: §7.3: a Drawer implies the page behind it is still usable. A blocking commit is a Modal.

items.do: Add wf-overlay-visible to show the overlay
items.dont: Leave it off and wonder why nothing renders
items.why: wf-overlay is visibility:hidden / opacity:0 by default; wf-overlay-visible is what fades the scrim in and animates the surface into place.

items.title: Modals trap focus; drawers don't have to
items.detail: DESIGN.md §6.5: a wf-modal traps focus — Tab cycles inside it, Esc closes it, and focus returns to the invoking element on close. A wf-drawer does not have to trap focus, because the page behind it is often still meant to be scannable. Wire your prototype's focus management to match the surface you chose.

items.title: The scrim is a sibling, not a parent
items.detail: wf-overlay-backdrop is an absolutely-positioned sibling of the modal/drawer inside wf-overlay (z-index 1000), not a wrapper around it. The surface sits at z-index 1001, above the backdrop. Forget the backdrop div and you get a floating panel with no dim behind it.

items.title: wf-overlay-visible drives every entrance animation
items.detail: The class doesn't just toggle visibility — descendant selectors like .wf-overlay-visible .wf-modal and .wf-overlay-visible .wf-drawer-right reset the surface's transform to zero. Without it the modal stays scaled-down and the drawer stays translated off-screen, even if you force the scrim visible some other way.

items.title: wf-modal-confirm is a modifier, not a component
items.detail: It only restyles wf-modal's body (center-stacked) and footer (centered). You still need wf-modal wf-modal-sm on the same element, and you still build it inside a wf-overlay with a backdrop. There is no standalone confirm element.

items.title: wf-confirm-icon-danger and -error are identical
items.detail: Both map to the same #fef2f2 tint and --wf-color-error foreground in feedback.css. Pick the name that reads clearest in context; they render the same.

items.title: Tooltips ride at z-index 3000 and ignore pointer events
items.detail: wf-tooltip sits above even toasts (2000) and overlays (1000), and is pointer-events:none so it never intercepts clicks meant for the trigger. wf-tooltip-visible is a wireframe-only crutch — it pins the tooltip open and does not represent real hover behavior.

items.title: Default tooltips don't wrap
items.detail: wf-tooltip is white-space:nowrap, so a long string blows out horizontally off-screen. Add wf-tooltip-multiline to cap it at 200px and allow wrapping the moment the hint runs past a few words.
